questions.question_text: Two siblings inherit a property. Without any agreement specifying otherwise, how do they hold title?
distractors: As joint tenants
As community property
In sole ownership by the eldest
questions.explanation: In Wisconsin, when multiple owners take title without specifying the type of ownership, the default is tenancy in common. Joint tenancy requires specific language and the four unities.

questions.question_text: What are the four unities required for joint tenancy in Wisconsin?
distractors: Consent, consideration, capacity, and legality
Intent, delivery, acceptance, and consideration
Time, place, manner, and consideration
questions.explanation: Joint tenancy requires the four unities: time (acquired at the same time), title (same deed), interest (equal shares), and possession (equal right to use the whole property).

questions.question_text: One joint tenant in Wisconsin dies. What happens to their interest?
questions.correct_answer: It passes to the surviving joint tenant(s)
distractors: It passes to their heirs through probate
It reverts to the state of Wisconsin
It is divided among all co-owners equally
questions.explanation: Joint tenancy includes the right of survivorship — when one joint tenant dies, their interest automatically passes to the surviving joint tenant(s) without going through probate.

questions.question_text: A tenant in common sells their interest to a third party. What happens to the other owners' interests?
questions.correct_answer: They are unaffected and continue to own their shares
distractors: All owners must consent to the sale first
The other owners must buy the interest first
All ownership reverts to tenancy at will
questions.explanation: Tenants in common can independently sell, mortgage, or transfer their interest without consent from other owners. The other owners' interests remain unchanged, and the new buyer becomes a co-tenant.

questions.question_text: Which ownership form does NOT include right of survivorship?
distractors: Joint tenancy
Survivorship marital property
Marital property with survivorship
questions.explanation: Tenancy in common does not include right of survivorship. When a tenant in common dies, their interest passes through their will or by intestate succession, not automatically to the other co-owners.

questions.question_text: Wisconsin is one of how many community property states in the U.S.?
distractors: 3 states
15 states
25 states
questions.explanation: Wisconsin adopted the Uniform Marital Property Act in 1986, making it one of only 9 community property states. Property acquired during marriage is presumed to be marital (community) property.

questions.question_text: Under Chapter 766, a couple buys a house during their marriage using earnings from one spouse's job. This property is classified as:
distractors: Individual property of the earning spouse
Tenancy in common property
Personal property of both spouses
questions.explanation: Under the Marital Property Act, all property acquired during marriage by either spouse is presumed to be marital property. Earnings during marriage are marital property, so property purchased with those earnings is also marital.

questions.question_text: Which property is classified as individual rather than marital property in Wisconsin?
questions.correct_answer: Property owned by a spouse before marriage
distractors: Property purchased during marriage with savings
Property acquired with a mortgage during marriage
A home bought as the marital residence
questions.explanation: Under Chapter 766, individual property includes property owned before marriage, gifts received by one spouse, and inheritances. These remain individual property unless commingled with marital property.

questions.question_text: One spouse inherits a cabin from a parent during the marriage. Under the Marital Property Act, this is:
questions.correct_answer: Individual property of the inheriting spouse
distractors: Marital property of both spouses
Community property requiring both names on title
Joint tenancy property automatically
questions.explanation: Inheritances are classified as individual property under the Marital Property Act, even if received during marriage. The inheriting spouse retains sole ownership unless they commingle it with marital property.

questions.question_text: All property of married spouses in Wisconsin is presumed to be which type?
distractors: Individual property
Joint tenancy property
Tenancy in common property
questions.explanation: Under the Marital Property Act (Chapter 766), all property of spouses is presumed to be marital property unless classified otherwise. This presumption can be overcome for property owned before marriage, gifts, and inheritances.

questions.question_text: A married person wants to sell their marital home in Wisconsin. What must occur?
questions.correct_answer: Both spouses must generally consent to the sale
distractors: Only the spouse whose name is on the title
Only the spouse who earned the down payment
Either spouse can sell without the other's consent
questions.explanation: Under the Marital Property Act, both spouses must generally consent to sell or encumber marital real property. This protects both spouses' interests in community property.

questions.question_text: Can spouses in Wisconsin change the default community property classification?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, through a marital property agreement under §766.58
distractors: No, the classification cannot be changed
Only by court order during divorce
Only by filing with the Register of Deeds
questions.explanation: Spouses may execute a marital property agreement under §766.58 to alter the default community property classification. However, additional legal formalities may apply when real property is involved.

questions.question_text: A husband signs a listing contract but his wife refuses. Can the property be sold?
questions.correct_answer: Generally no, both spouses must consent for marital real property
distractors: Yes, one spouse's signature is sufficient
Yes, the listing agent can proceed with one signature
Only if the property is titled in the husband's name alone
questions.explanation: Even if title is in one spouse's name, if it is marital property, both spouses must generally consent to the sale. The listing agent should not proceed without both signatures.

questions.question_text: What happens to marital property when one spouse dies in Wisconsin?
questions.correct_answer: The surviving spouse has rights to the marital property
distractors: All marital property goes to probate court
The deceased spouse's half goes to the state
Marital property is divided among all heirs equally
questions.explanation: The surviving spouse has rights to marital property upon the death of the other spouse under the Marital Property Act. The exact distribution depends on the estate plan and whether survivorship rights were established.

questions.question_text: A licensee is preparing a listing for marital property. What should they verify?
questions.correct_answer: That both spouses will sign the listing contract
distractors: That the property is in both names on the deed
That neither spouse has filed for divorce
That the mortgage is current
questions.explanation: Since both spouses generally must consent to sell marital real property, the licensee should verify that both will sign the listing contract. This is true even if only one spouse's name is on the deed.
